text:
MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — HEAD AND NECK SURVEILLANCE PROTOCOL

Patient: Margaret Chen
Clinical Indication: 54-year-old female with history of HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0, treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions with weekly cisplatin); 2-year post-treatment surveillance MRI. Prior imaging available for comparison (MRI neck at 12 months post-treatment).

TECHNIQUE:
MRI of the neck performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner utilizing a dedicated head and neck surface coil array. Sequences obtained include: axial and coronal T1-weighted spin echo (pre- and post-contrast), axial T2-weighted with fat saturation, axial and sagittal STIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with ADC mapping, and dynamic contrast-enhanced sequences following administration of 0.1 mmol/kg of gadobutrol (Gadavist) IV. Coverage from the skull base through the thoracic inlet. Multiplanar reformats generated. Images reviewed in comparison to prior MRI neck performed at 12 months post-treatment and the post-treatment MRI obtained at 3 months following completion of chemoradiotherapy.

FINDINGS:

Primary Treatment Site — Left Base of Tongue and Oropharynx:
The left base of tongue demonstrates stable post-radiation mucosal changes with mild diffuse T2 signal hyperintensity and minimal mucosal thickening, unchanged from the 12-month surveillance study. There is no discrete mass, focal area of restricted diffusion, or pathologically enhancing lesion identified within the left base of tongue, vallecula, or adjacent oropharyngeal mucosa. The previously treated primary tumor bed shows expected post-radiation fibrotic remodeling without any worrisome nodular or irregular enhancement pattern. The deep tongue musculature is intact with no infiltrative abnormality. The midline is well-preserved with no contralateral extension. No ulceration or frank necrosis is identified.

Oropharynx and Adjacent Structures:
The right base of tongue appears normal. The soft palate, tonsillar fossae bilaterally, posterior pharyngeal wall, and posterior oropharyngeal mucosa are unremarkable. The pre-epiglottic space is patent. The epiglottis is normal in morphology. No mass effect upon the airway. Uvula and palatine tonsils are not enlarged. The retropharyngeal space is clear.

Cervical Lymph Node Stations:
The small residual soft tissue focus within the left level IIA nodal station, previously measuring 8 mm on the 12-month post-treatment MRI, has resolved completely and is no longer discernible on the current examination. There is no pathologically enlarged, morphologically abnormal, or abnormally enhancing lymph node identified in the left or right levels I through VI bilaterally. Specifically reviewed: bilateral levels IA, IB, IIA, IIB, III, IV, V (including VA/VB), and level VI (prelaryngeal/pretracheal); all are within normal limits. No evidence of extranodal extension or nodal necrosis. No new adenopathy in the retropharyngeal, parapharyngeal, or retrostyloid spaces.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
Mild diffuse radiation-related fibrotic changes are present in the subcutaneous soft tissues of the bilateral neck, most prominent in the left anterolateral neck corresponding to the prior high-dose radiation field. These fibrotic changes are stable compared to prior study and are expected sequelae of head and neck irradiation. No focal fluid collection, seroma, or abscess. The fat planes are intact. The floor of mouth is unremarkable. No evidence of radiation-induced soft tissue necrosis.

Mandible and Osseous Structures:
No lytic, blastic, or destructive osseous lesion. No pathological fracture. No evidence of osteoradionecrosis of the mandible — cortical integrity is maintained and there is no marrow signal abnormality, periosteal reaction, or sequestrum. The cervical vertebrae show age-appropriate degenerative disc changes at C5-C6 and C6-C7 without significant neural foraminal compromise. No suspicious vertebral lesions.

Thyroid and Parathyroid Glands:
The thyroid gland is heterogeneous in signal and mildly reduced in volume, consistent with prior radiation-induced thyroiditis/atrophy — findings stable compared to prior study and consistent with the patient's known history of radiation-induced hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine replacement. No dominant thyroid nodule or focal mass. No parathyroid lesion identified.

Salivary Glands:
Bilateral parotid glands are mildly atrophic and fatty replaced, in keeping with expected post-radiation parotid gland changes. The submandibular glands are similarly mildly atrophied bilaterally. These findings are stable and consistent with prior radiation-induced salivary gland injury. No mass or ductal obstruction identified. Wharton's ducts appear patent.

Vascular Structures:
The common carotid arteries, internal carotid arteries, and external carotid arteries are patent bilaterally. There is mild mural irregularity and intimal thickening of the bilateral common and internal carotid arteries consistent with mild atherosclerotic disease and superimposed radiation-induced vasculopathy; this is stable without significant interval progression compared to the prior MRI. No high-grade stenosis. No carotid body tumor. The internal jugular veins are patent bilaterally without evidence of thrombosis.

Larynx and Hypopharynx:
The larynx is structurally intact. The true and false vocal cords are symmetric in appearance. No subglottic or supraglottic mass. The aryepiglottic folds are unremarkable. The pyriform sinuses are symmetric and patent. The hypopharyngeal mucosa is intact. No tracheal deviation or compression.

Skull Base and Parapharyngeal Spaces:
No abnormality at the skull base. Bilateral foramina are intact with no evidence of perineural spread or neural involvement. The parapharyngeal, masticator, and carotid spaces are unremarkable bilaterally. The pterygoid muscles are symmetric.

Incidental Findings:
Small bilateral submandibular gland retention cysts (likely mucous retention cysts), measuring up to 4 mm, of no clinical significance. Mild mucosal thickening of the left maxillary sinus compatible with chronic sinusitis.

IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence at the left base of tongue primary site or in any cervical nodal station, now two years after completion of definitive chemoradiotherapy. The treatment bed demonstrates stable post-radiation fibrotic changes without any suspicious enhancing lesion, restricted diffusion, or morphologic features to suggest residual or recurrent tumor.
2. The small residual soft tissue focus previously noted at the left level IIA station has completely resolved and is no longer visible, further supporting a durable complete response.
3. Expected and stable post-radiation changes throughout the neck, including soft tissue fibrosis, parotid and submandibular gland atrophy, and thyroid gland volume reduction consistent with radiation-induced injury.
4. Stable mild atherosclerotic changes of the bilateral carotid arteries with superimposed radiation vasculopathy; no hemodynamically significant progression.
5. No evidence of radiation-induced mandibular osteoradionecrosis.
6. Small bilateral submandibular retention cysts — incidental, no follow-up needed.

This examination was reviewed and interpreted by the attending radiologist.

Attending Radiologist: [Name on file]
Dictated and Signed Electronically
